summary: Drew DeVault reflects on a devastating year of personal trauma and burnout, much of it connected to failures within hacker communities to address sexual violence and predatory behavior. He describes discovering that communities he loved harbored rapists and pedophiles, and that speaking up about these issues brought further trauma rather than resolution. He argues that the hacker community's tolerance of casual sexism, misogyny, and racism has enabled a pervasive, silent culture of abuse. He calls on community members to believe victims, remove harmful people, and support codes of conduct, while announcing his own withdrawal from in-person hacker spaces.
key_points: Multiple hacker communities DeVault was part of failed to address rape and predatory behavior, keeping serious crimes silent
Speaking truth about abuse within communities brought personal trauma and upheaval rather than support or resolution
The hacker ethic of tolerance and inclusion has been co-opted as cover for tolerating abusers while marginalizing women, queer people, and people of color
These problems are pervasive and silent across hacker communities, not isolated incidents
Community members, especially men with social capital, must actively stand up for victims and enforce codes of conduct
money_quotes: The hacker community has been the home I never had, but I'm not really feeling at home here right now.
I am a principled person, and I stand for what is right; I spoke the truth and it brought me and my loved ones agonizing stress and trauma and shook our community to the core.
What began as a wonderful reminder of what the hacker community can be became a PTSD episode and a reminder that rape culture is fucking everywhere.

Our communities have tolerated casual sexism and misogyny and transphobia and racism and actual fucking rapists, and stamped down on women and queer people and brown people in our spaces with a smile on our face and a fucked-up facsimile of tolerance and inclusion as a cornerstone of the hacker ethic.
If there's one thing I came to understand this year, it's that these problems are pervasive and silent.
This destroys communities. It is destroying our communities.
And for fuck's sake, don't bitch about that code of conduct that someone wants to add to your community.
Be there for the people you love, and love more people, and be there for them, too.
key_insight: The hacker community's culture of permissive tolerance has silently enabled sexual violence and predatory behavior, and only active, principled intervention — not passive inclusion rhetoric — can fix it.
